title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — July 7, 2026
headline: Venezuela's earthquake death toll hits 3,535 on day 13, and a state response called "the last responder" stays just under the threshold for a score change.
summary: July 7, 2026 is a confirmation-dominant cycle: all 15 assessed countries hold at their published scores. Zero new score-change proposals were filed, and the queue of pending changes stays empty for a second straight day. Venezuela's earthquake death toll reached 3,535 people on day 13, up from 2,645 four days earlier. A forensic pathologist told CNN the real number is "not even a third" of what has been reported. A regional aid expert said plainly that Venezuela's government "has been the last responder," with citizens organizing their own rescues. Recalculating Venezuela's score with this evidence would move it down 2.7 points — real, documented decline, but just under the 5-point bar the benchmark requires before it changes a published score. Cuba joins tonight's watch list for the first time this cycle: a third nationwide blackout in six months forced hospitals to cancel tens of thousands of surgeries. Sudan holds at 0 of 100 as the United Nations issues a "red alert" over drone strikes on the city of El Obeid — 15 strikes in three weeks have killed more than 45 civilians. Iran holds at 2.5 of 100 as security officials bar the country's new leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, from his own father's burial for fear of assassination. A years-old disagreement over how Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) are scored for comparable military-government conduct has now gone two full weeks without resolution — long enough that this briefing is flagging it as an open methodology question rather than routine business.

pipeline.elSalvadorHandling: El Salvador's downgrade to 15.0 of 100 (Critical band) remains applied from 2026-07-05. El Salvador was not in tonight's priority cluster. No carry-forward action.

topSignals.title: Venezuela's Earthquake Toll Hits 3,535 on Day 13 — a Response Called "the Last Responder" Stays Just Under the Threshold for a Score Change; Cuba's Third Blackout in Six Months Adds a New Watch
topSignals.whyItMatters: Venezuela's government response to its earthquake shows the clearest sign of decline the benchmark has tracked all month — real evidence, just short of the bar for a score change. Cuba's third blackout in six months raises the same kind of concern.
topSignals.description: Venezuela holds at 18 of 100 in the Critical band. The earthquake death toll reached 3,535 people on day 13 (July 7), up from 2,645 four days earlier and from 235 on June 26 — a climb steep enough that a forensic pathologist told CNN, speaking anonymously out of fear of retaliation, that the true toll is "not even a third of what is actually there." Carolina Jimenez, president of the Washington Office on Latin America, put it directly: "In a government in any other country, the first responder should be the state... In the case of Venezuela, the state has been the last responder." Citizens have organized their own rescues and aid distribution. Recalculating Venezuela's three most-affected areas — public awareness of the true toll, government action, and accountability for it — would move the score down to 15.3, a decline of 2.7 points. That is real, documented decline. It falls just short of the 5-point change the benchmark requires before updating a published score, so Venezuela is confirmed at 18 of 100 with the strongest downward watch flag issued this cycle. Cuba holds at 32.8 of 100 in the Developing band and enters tonight's watch list for the first time. A nationwide blackout on July 6 was Cuba's third in six months; tens of thousands of scheduled surgeries were canceled and public transportation mostly stopped. Cuba makes only about 40 percent of the fuel it needs, and residents held rare, sustained street protests demanding the power be restored. Recalculating Cuba's government-action and system-capacity scores for this collapse would move the score down to 29.7, a decline of 3.1 points — again real, but under the 5-point threshold. A data-tracking error had also listed Cuba's working score too low (27.8 instead of the correct 32.8); that error is now fixed. Confirmed at 18 of 100 for Venezuela and 32.8 of 100 for Cuba; confidence: high for both.

evidence.quote: the state has been the last responder
evidence.claim: WOLA president Carolina Jimenez on Venezuela's earthquake response: the state, rather than being the first responder, has been the last.

evidence.quote: not even a third of what is actually there
evidence.claim: A forensic pathologist, speaking anonymously to CNN out of fear of retaliation, on the credibility of Venezuela's official earthquake death toll.

Al Jazeera July 7, 2026: Cuba suffered its third nationwide power blackout in six months on July 6; tens of thousands of surgeries were canceled and public transportation largely halted amid a fuel shortage tied to the U.S. fuel blockade.

topSignals.title: Sudan Holds at 0 of 100 as the UN Issues a "Red Alert" Over Drone Strikes on El Obeid; DRC Holds at 2.3 as Ebola Passes 1,500 Cases With No Vaccine; Afghanistan Holds at 0 on the Gender-Apartheid Floor
topSignals.whyItMatters: Sudan's siege of El Obeid is drawing direct comparisons to the massacre at El Fasher — the event that first triggered a crimes-against-humanity finding. The Democratic Republic of the Congo's Ebola outbreak has no approved vaccine and is still growing.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at 0 of 100 — the absolute floor. The UN's human rights chief, Volker Turk, sounded a "red alert" over Rapid Support Forces drone strikes on El Obeid: 15 strikes in three weeks have killed more than 45 civilians, including 10 straight days of strikes that killed more than 50 people and hit markets, schools, fuel stations, and water infrastructure. Al Jazeera asked directly on July 6: "Could this be Sudan's next El Fasher?" — a reference to the North Darfur city where Amnesty International documented Rapid Support Forces crimes against humanity, including mass killings and systematic rape, on July 1. About 500,000 civilians are trapped under the El Obeid siege. This harm is caused by the Rapid Support Forces, a non-state armed group, not the Sudanese government directly — but Sudan's floor score reflects the government's own prior failure to protect civilians and its refusal to accept a ceasefire. The Democratic Republic of the Congo holds at 2.3 of 100 — just above the floor. Its Ebola outbreak has reached 1,561 confirmed cases and 506 deaths, with the Ituri region hit hardest. There is no approved vaccine or treatment for this strain of Ebola (called Bundibugyo), and fighting in South Kivu is displacing people and blocking aid workers' access. Afghanistan holds at 0 of 100. Its floor score rests on the Taliban's formal gender-apartheid policies — laws restricting women's education, work, and movement — and is now compounded by the first-ever direct Taliban drone strike into Pakistani territory, launched July 1. Confirmed at 0 of 100 for Sudan and Afghanistan; 2.3 of 100 for DRC; confidence: high for all three.

evidence.quote: Could this be Sudan's next El Fasher?
evidence.claim: Al Jazeera's framing of the RSF drone-strike siege on El Obeid, drawing a direct comparison to the El Fasher massacre that produced a crimes-against-humanity finding.

United Nations, July 2026: UN human rights chief Volker Turk sounded a "red alert" over 15 documented RSF drone strikes on El Obeid over three weeks, killing more than 45 civilians and striking markets, schools, and water infrastructure.

World Health Organization, July 2026: DRC's Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak has reached 1,561 confirmed cases and 506 deaths, concentrated in Ituri province; no approved vaccine or treatment exists for this strain.

topSignals.title: Iran Holds at 2.5 of 100 as Security Officials Bar New Leader Mojtaba Khamenei From His Father's Burial Over Assassination Fears; Russia Holds at 0 as Kyiv Strikes Kill Dozens and the EU Sanctions Six Over the Navalny Poisoning
topSignals.whyItMatters: Iran's execution rate is at its highest in 37 years, even as the country buries its Supreme Leader. Russia's daily civilian casualty rate in Ukraine is now roughly 20 percent higher than the same period last year.
topSignals.description: Iran holds at 2.5 of 100, just above the floor. Iran's new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, was barred by his own security officials from attending his father Ali Khamenei's July 9 burial in Mashhad — officials feared Israel could kill him or track his location if he appeared in public. State funeral ceremonies continue through July 9 across four cities, with 15 to 20 million mourners expected. More than 108 executions were carried out in June alone, part of a year-to-date total exceeding 784 — the highest rate Iran has recorded in 37 years. The leadership transition is a political event, not a change in how Iran treats its own people, so it does not move the score. Russia holds at 0 of 100 — the absolute floor. A missile-and-drone assault on Kyiv on July 5 and 6 killed 19 people in the city and 8 more in the surrounding region; a separate barrage killed 21 more civilians. United Nations monitors report Ukrainian civilian casualties now averaging about 170 people per day in July, roughly 20 percent higher than the same period in 2025. Separately, the European Union sanctioned six Russian individuals this month for developing the chemical weapon found in samples from opposition leader Alexei Navalny's body after his death, and renewed its broader Russia sanctions for another year. Ukraine holds at 50 of 100 in the Functional band: the harm inflicted on Ukrainian civilians is Russia's conduct, not Ukraine's, and Ukraine's own civilian-protection response — sheltering and evacuating people during the strikes — is scored on its own terms. Confirmed at 2.5 of 100 for Iran, 0 of 100 for Russia, and 50 of 100 for Ukraine; confidence: high for all three.

evidence.claim: Times of Israel, July 2026: Iranian security officials barred new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei from attending his father's July 9 burial in Mashhad, citing fear that Israel could kill or locate him if he appeared in public.

OHCHR Ukraine Monitoring Mission, July 2026: Ukrainian civilian-casualty rates in July are averaging roughly 170 per day, about 20 percent higher than the same period in 2025, driven by increased long-range Russian missile and drone strikes on urban areas.

Council of the European Union, June 25, 2026: the EU sanctioned six Russian individuals involved in developing the chemical weapon (epibatidine) found in samples from Alexei Navalny's body, and extended broader Russia sanctions for another 12 months.

topSignals.title: Israel Holds at 0 of 100 on UN-Documented West Bank Settler Violence and a Lebanon Strike That Killed Four; China Holds at 19.5 as a New Assimilation Law Takes Effect and a Tibetan Activist Dies by Self-Immolation at the UN
topSignals.whyItMatters: United Nations investigators say Israeli authorities are directing or enabling settler violence that has killed 13 Palestinians in five months. In China, a new law requiring Mandarin-only instruction has already produced a death in protest outside UN Headquarters.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at 0 of 100 — the absolute floor. United Nations experts documented 13 Palestinians killed and about 500 injured in West Bank settler violence over five months, with more than 2,300 people displaced in 2026 alone. The UN's human rights office described the violence as "coordinated, strategic, largely unchallenged," with Israeli authorities "directing, participating in, or enabling it." On July 6, an Israeli strike on a vehicle in southern Lebanon killed four civilians, including a school principal, her mother, a domestic worker, and a Syrian citizen. Six countries — the UK, Australia, Canada, France, New Zealand, and Norway — have sanctioned networks that finance settler violence, and the International Criminal Court's arrest warrants for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and former defense minister Yoav Gallant still stand; the United States retaliated in June by sanctioning two more ICC judges. Lebanon holds at 17.2 of 100: the July 6 strike is scored against Israel as the acting state, not against Lebanon, whose own conduct in the window was passive inaction amid ongoing ceasefire violations — already reflected in its score. China holds at 19.5 of 100, just half a point inside the Critical band. China's Ethnic Unity and Progress Promotion Law took effect July 1, requiring Mandarin as the primary language in schools and government offices and mandating curricula that instill loyalty to the Chinese Communist Party. UN human rights experts warned in April the law carries "serious implications" for Tibetan, Uyghur, and Mongol cultural and religious autonomy, and Amnesty International said it will "entrench assimilation of minority groups." On July 1, Tibetan activist Lobsang Palden set himself on fire near United Nations Headquarters in New York in protest of the law and later died. Confirmed at 0 of 100 for Israel, 17.2 of 100 for Lebanon, and 19.5 of 100 for China; confidence: high for all three.

evidence.claim: UN human rights office characterization of West Bank settler violence, with Israeli authorities described as directing, participating in, or enabling it.
Al Jazeera, July 6, 2026: an Israeli strike on a civilian vehicle in southern Lebanon killed four people, including a school principal, her mother, a foreign domestic worker, and a Syrian citizen.

Amnesty International, June 2026: China's Ethnic Unity and Progress Promotion Law, in force since July 1, will entrench assimilation of Tibetan, Uyghur, and Mongol minority groups; UN experts warned in April of serious implications for linguistic, cultural, and religious autonomy.

topSignals.title: A Two-Week-Old Scoring Disagreement Between Mali and Burkina Faso Gets Flagged as an Open Methodology Question; Nigeria Holds at 18 as 54.8 Million Face Hunger Across West Africa; the United States Joins Tonight's List at 17.5
topSignals.whyItMatters: Mali and Burkina Faso are both run by military governments with comparable records of failing to protect civilians, yet Mali's score sits nearly twice as high as Burkina Faso's — and that gap has gone unresolved for two weeks.
topSignals.description: Mali holds at 12.5 of 100. Fighters from JNIM, an al-Qaeda-linked armed group, renewed a coordinated offensive on July 4 after a nearly two-month pause, striking multiple towns, while the group's blockade of fuel and food into the capital, Bamako, continues. A standing question about whether Mali's score is properly calibrated against Burkina Faso's — a comparable military government scored at 6.3 of 100 for similarly documented state collapse — was first raised on June 24 and remains unresolved 14 days later. This briefing is flagging it explicitly: a gap this size, between two peer governments with comparable conduct, run for two weeks without a decision is a question for the benchmark's own methodology team, not something a single nightly check can resolve on its own. Nigeria holds at 18 of 100: roughly 35 million people face food insecurity, with the country's northeast the epicenter. A joint UN food-agency briefing on July 9 will update a regional figure showing up to 54.8 million people across West Africa and the Sahel at risk during the June-to-August hunger season, with food-aid funding cut more than 45 percent in Niger and Mali compared with last year. The United States joins tonight's list of scored countries at 17.5 of 100, still in the Critical band it entered on June 9. The picture is genuinely mixed: the Supreme Court upheld birthright citizenship on June 30, a check on the executive branch, while separately upholding limits on daily asylum applications and allowing termination of Temporary Protected Status for Haitian and Syrian nationals. A separate housing-policy change caps rental-assistance funding for formerly homeless people at 30 percent of levels used previously, down from about 87 percent — advocates warn this could disrupt housing assistance for more than 170,000 people. Confirmed at 12.5 of 100 for Mali, 18 of 100 for Nigeria, and 17.5 of 100 for the United States; confidence: high for all three.

evidence.claim: Compassion Benchmark assessment record: a cross-peer calibration question between Mali (12.5 of 100) and Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) — two military governments with comparably documented state-collapse conduct — was routed to coordinator-level review on June 24 and remains open 14 days later.

Food and Agriculture Organization / World Food Programme, July 2026: up to 54.8 million people across West Africa and the Sahel face acute food insecurity during the June-August 2026 lean season; food-assistance funding has been cut more than 45 percent in Niger and Mali compared with the prior year.

Shelterforce, 2026: HUD's 2026 Continuum of Care policy caps supportive-services rental assistance at 30 percent of prior funding levels, down from roughly 87 percent, moving away from Housing First models; advocates project disruption to housing assistance for more than 170,000 formerly homeless people.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Due-process restoration, civil-society reinstatement, and reversal of the indefinite re-election amendment remain the path back toward 20 of 100. The 2027 election under the amendment is the next scheduled test.
boundaryWatch.note: El Salvador sits at 15.0 of 100 in the Critical band, 5.0 points below the boundary with the Developing band. Not assessed tonight; second cycle since the July 5 downgrade was applied.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause — a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad — is the next threshold event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: China sits at 19.5 of 100, half a point inside the Critical band. The new assimilation law took effect July 1 with no confirmed enforcement action yet documented abroad.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable August 2 — 26 days away.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits at 59.1 of 100, 0.9 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A labor board ruling on the unfair-labor-practice charge tied to Apple's closure of its first unionized store is the next scored event to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits at 59.4 of 100, 0.6 points below the Established band. The labor board review has not yet concluded. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A durable ceasefire combined with the Lebanese Armed Forces taking sovereign control of southern Lebanon and delivering reconstruction would together move Lebanon toward 20 of 100.
boundaryWatch.note: Lebanon sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the boundary with the Developing band. The July 6 strike that killed four in southern Lebanon is Israeli conduct, scored against Israel, not Lebanon.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently verified deliberate targeting of civilians in a cross-border strike would move Pakistan's score down; durable relief for detained protesters and Afghan refugees facing deportation would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Pakistan sits at 17.2 of 100, 2.8 points below the Developing band. Domestic crackdowns this cycle — mass preventive detention, a 27-channel media ban, continued Afghan-refugee deportation — reinforce, but do not cross, the existing score.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, is the next event that would move Venezuela's score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Venezuela sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points above the Critical floor and 2.0 points below the Developing band. Day 13 earthquake response evidence would move the score to 15.3 if applied — a 2.7-point decline, just under the 5-point threshold for a change. Strongest downward watch of the cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration in Borno state combined with documented aid obstruction, or realized food-assistance cuts from the July 9 regional briefing, are the next scored events to watch.
boundaryWatch.note: Nigeria sits at 18.0 of 100, 2.0 points below the Developing band. Roughly 35 million people face food insecurity; the crisis was already priced into the June 19 score.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors in Haut-Uele, Ituri, or North Kivu would be the trigger to move toward the absolute floor. The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC sits at 2.3 of 100, 2.3 points above the absolute floor. Its Ebola outbreak has reached 1,561 cases and 506 deaths with no approved vaccine for this strain.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A documented lethal crackdown on mourners or political rivals during the funeral window (through July 9) would move Iran's score toward the floor.
boundaryWatch.note: Iran sits at 2.5 of 100, 2.5 points above the absolute floor. Mojtaba Khamenei was barred from his father's July 9 burial over assassination fears; more than 108 executions were recorded in June alone.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented expulsions of Bengali Muslim and Rohingya populations, and continued digital surveillance targeting minority groups, remain under watch. Not assessed tonight.
boundaryWatch.note: India sits at 15.6 of 100, 0.6 points above the absolute floor, in its twenty-third consecutive monitoring cycle. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A formal famine declaration covering Buur Hakaba or Baidoa, combined with documented government aid obstruction, is the next scored trigger. The famine-risk window runs through September 2026.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight; fourteenth floor-proximity cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented discriminatory denial of emergency protection to stateless bidoon or migrant workers during wartime would move Kuwait's score down; durable expansion of bidoon rights would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Kuwait sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight; ninth boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A coordinator-level review comparing Mali's conduct against Burkina Faso's (scored 6.3 of 100 for comparable state collapse) is the next scored event needed to resolve this gap. No review date has been set.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali sits at 12.5 of 100. A calibration question against peer country Burkina Faso has been open for 14 consecutive days without a coordinator decision — the longest open item currently tracked.

boundaryWatch.trigger: The outcome of the Justice Department's False Claims Act complaint or the Barrows v. Humana class action over AI-driven claim denials would confirm the current Developing-band placement either way.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana sits at 35.2 of 100, 4.8 points below the Functional band. Not assessed tonight; neither legal matter has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Forced cuts that measurably reduce student or staff support would count against the score; abandoning its academic-freedom stance would as well.
boundaryWatch.note: Princeton sits at 57.8 of 100, 2.2 points below the Established band. Not assessed tonight.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Independently documented deliberate targeting of Palestinian civilians by Palestinian authorities themselves, or systematic diversion of aid, would move the score down.
boundaryWatch.note: Palestine sits at 25.0 of 100, 5.0 points above the Critical band. Not assessed tonight; harm from Israeli strikes continues to be scored against Israel, not Palestine.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Documented atrocities by Haitian state security forces, or deliberate relief obstruction, would move the score down. Verified state-led civilian protection would move it up.
boundaryWatch.note: Haiti sits at 4.7 of 100. Not assessed tonight. Temporary Protected Status work authorization for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States expires July 10 — that is U.S. conduct, not scored against Haiti.

boundaryWatch.trigger: A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond tens of thousands of canceled surgeries, is the next event that would move Cuba's score toward a formal change.
boundaryWatch.note: Cuba sits at 32.8 of 100 after a data-tracking correction from an incorrectly recorded 27.8. A third nationwide blackout in six months (July 6) canceled tens of thousands of surgeries; recalculating for this evidence would move the score to 29.7, a 3.1-point decline, just under the threshold for a change. First cycle in this watch list.

emergingRisks.risk: Venezuela's earthquake death toll has climbed from 235 people on June 26 to 3,535 on July 7, a pace steep enough that a forensic pathologist told CNN the real number is "not even a third" of what has been reported.
emergingRisks.detail: Recalculating Venezuela's awareness, action, and accountability performance for the disaster response would move the score to 15.3 — a 2.7-point decline, just under the 5-point threshold the benchmark requires before changing a published score. Independent confirmation of the undercount allegation is the next event that would cross that line.

emergingRisks.risk: Cuba's third nationwide blackout in six months forced hospitals to cancel tens of thousands of scheduled surgeries, and residents have held rare, sustained street protests.
emergingRisks.detail: Recalculating for the blackout would move Cuba's score to 29.7 — a 3.1-point decline, also just under the threshold for a change. A fourth blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure worse than the current figure, would be the next evidence point to watch.

emergingRisks.risk: Sudan's siege of El Obeid is now drawing direct public comparison to the El Fasher massacre that produced a crimes-against-humanity finding, with 500,000 civilians trapped.
emergingRisks.detail: Sudan's government has not accepted a ceasefire. Documentation from UN humanitarian agencies on how the siege evolves is the next checkpoint for whether El Obeid follows El Fasher toward a formal atrocity finding.

emergingRisks.risk: Iran's funeral window for Ali Khamenei closes July 9, with his son and successor barred from attending in person over assassination fears.
emergingRisks.detail: More than 108 executions were recorded in June alone, the highest monthly rate in 37 years. A documented lethal crackdown on mourners or political rivals during the funeral window would be a new scored event. U.S.-Iran talks resume July 11.

emergingRisks.risk: A calibration question comparing Mali's score (12.5 of 100) against peer country Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) for comparably documented military-government conduct has now gone 14 consecutive days without a coordinator decision.
emergingRisks.detail: Two full weeks without a resolution date is longer than any other open methodology question currently tracked. A coordinator-level review with a set timeline is the next step needed to close this gap and preserve consistent scoring across comparable governments.

emergingRisks.risk: The Democratic Republic of the Congo's Ebola outbreak has reached 1,561 cases and 506 deaths with no approved vaccine, while fighting in South Kivu restricts humanitarian access.
emergingRisks.detail: The natural seasonal peak for this outbreak runs through July 31. Documented government obstruction of World Health Organization response corridors would be the trigger toward the absolute floor.

emergingRisks.risk: Temporary Protected Status work authorization for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States expires July 10, even though Haiti itself was not part of tonight's assessment.
emergingRisks.detail: This is United States conduct, not Haitian conduct, and continues to be tracked as a forward trigger for the U.S. score even while Haiti rotates out of the nightly priority list.

emergingRisks.risk: China's new Mandarin-only assimilation law took effect July 1 with no confirmed enforcement action documented abroad yet, even as a Tibetan activist died protesting it outside UN Headquarters.
emergingRisks.detail: A confirmed prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad under the law's diaspora-liability clause is the specific threshold event that would move China's score below the Critical band boundary it currently sits half a point inside of.

emergingRisks.risk: A joint UN food-agency briefing on July 9 will update a regional figure showing up to 54.8 million people across West Africa and the Sahel at risk of hunger, with funding cuts exceeding 45 percent in Niger and Mali.
emergingRisks.detail: Nigeria's score already reflects the June 19 hunger-crisis baseline. A formal famine declaration in Borno state with documented aid obstruction, or realized food-assistance cuts from the July 9 briefing, would be the next scored events.

sectorAlerts.sector: Latin America and the Caribbean — Venezuela's Disaster Response and Cuba's Grid Collapse
sectorAlerts.headline: Venezuela's earthquake death toll hits 3,535 on day 13 amid credible undercount concerns; Cuba's third blackout in six months cancels tens of thousands of surgeries. Both governments are confirmed at their published scores with the strongest downward watch flags issued tonight.
observations: Venezuela (18.0 of 100): the death toll climbed from 235 on June 26 to 3,535 on July 7. A forensic pathologist told CNN, speaking anonymously out of fear of retaliation, that the real number is "not even a third of what is actually there." A regional aid expert said the government "has been the last responder," with citizens organizing their own rescues.

Recalculating Venezuela's score for the documented response failure would move it to 15.3 — a 2.7-point decline. That is real evidence, but it falls just short of the 5-point threshold the benchmark requires before changing a published score.

Cuba (32.8 of 100) enters tonight's watch list for the first time. A third nationwide blackout in six months, on July 6, forced cancellation of tens of thousands of scheduled surgeries and halted most public transportation. A data-tracking error that had understated Cuba's working score (27.8 instead of 32.8) was corrected this cycle.

Recalculating for the blackout would move Cuba's score to 29.7 — a 3.1-point decline, also under the threshold. Both countries are documented as real, sub-threshold decline and are the leading candidates for a formal score change if the trend continues.

sectorAlerts.sector: Africa — Sudan's El Obeid Siege, DRC's Ebola Outbreak, and the Mali-Burkina Faso Calibration Question
sectorAlerts.headline: The UN sounds a "red alert" over drone strikes on Sudan's El Obeid; DRC's Ebola outbreak passes 1,500 cases with no vaccine; a 14-day-old scoring disagreement between Mali and Burkina Faso is formally flagged as unresolved.
observations: Sudan (0 of 100): 15 documented drone strikes on El Obeid over three weeks have killed more than 45 civilians, with about 500,000 people trapped under siege. Al Jazeera has drawn a direct comparison to the El Fasher massacre that produced a crimes-against-humanity finding on July 1.

The Democratic Republic of the Congo (2.3 of 100): its Ebola outbreak has reached 1,561 confirmed cases and 506 deaths, with no approved vaccine for this strain. Fighting in South Kivu is displacing people and restricting humanitarian access. The natural seasonal peak for the outbreak runs through July 31.

Mali (12.5 of 100): JNIM fighters renewed a coordinated offensive on July 4 and continue blockading fuel and food into the capital, Bamako. A standing question about whether Mali's score is properly calibrated against Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100) for comparable military-government conduct has now run 14 consecutive days without a coordinator decision.
This briefing formally flags the Mali-Burkina Faso gap as an open methodology question requiring a coordinator-level decision with a set timeline — not routine nightly business.

sectorAlerts.sector: Middle East and Iran — A Barred Burial, a Record Execution Rate, and a Contested Ceasefire
sectorAlerts.headline: Iran's new Supreme Leader is barred from his own father's burial over assassination fears; Israel holds at the floor on UN-documented settler violence and a Lebanon strike that killed four.
observations: Iran (2.5 of 100): Mojtaba Khamenei was barred by security officials from attending his father's July 9 burial in Mashhad. More than 108 executions were carried out in June alone, part of a year-to-date rate that is the highest in 37 years.

Israel (0 of 100): UN experts documented Israeli authorities "directing, participating in, or enabling" West Bank settler violence that has killed 13 Palestinians and injured about 500 over five months. A July 6 strike on a vehicle in southern Lebanon killed four civilians.

Lebanon (17.2 of 100) and Palestine (25.0 of 100, not assessed tonight) continue to have harm from Israeli strikes scored against Israel rather than against themselves, under the benchmark's rule that harm is scored against the party that causes it.

Six countries have sanctioned settler-violence financing networks, and International Criminal Court arrest warrants for Israel's prime minister and former defense minister still stand — accountability pressure remains entirely external to Israel's own government.

sectorAlerts.sector: Russia and Ukraine — Record Civilian Casualty Pace Continues
sectorAlerts.headline: Kyiv strikes on July 5-6 kill dozens as UN monitors report Ukraine's July civilian-casualty rate running about 20 percent above 2025 levels; the EU sanctions six over the Navalny poisoning.
observations: Russia (0 of 100): a missile-and-drone assault on Kyiv on July 5-6 killed 19 people in the city and 8 in the surrounding region; a separate barrage killed 21 more. UN monitors report Ukrainian civilian casualties averaging about 170 per day this month.

The European Union sanctioned six Russian individuals in June over developing the chemical weapon found in samples from opposition leader Alexei Navalny's body, and renewed its broader Russia sanctions for another year.

Ukraine (50.0 of 100, Functional band): the harm inflicted is Russia's conduct, scored against Russia. Ukraine's own civilian-protection response — sheltering and evacuating people during the strikes — is scored on its own terms and remains consistent with the Functional band.

sectorAlerts.sector: South Asia — Pakistan's Domestic Crackdown and the Afghanistan Border War
sectorAlerts.headline: Pakistan absorbs the first-ever direct Taliban drone strike into its territory while carrying out a domestic crackdown of its own; Afghanistan holds at the floor on its standing gender-apartheid basis.
observations: Pakistan (17.2 of 100): Taliban forces launched drone strikes into Balochistan on July 1 — the first direct aerial assault into Pakistani territory in this conflict. Domestically, Pakistani authorities carried out mass preventive detention of protesters, banned 27 media channels belonging to journalists and opposition figures, and confirmed continued deportation of long-recognized Afghan refugees.

Afghanistan (0 of 100): the same cross-border strikes are the Taliban's own conduct against Pakistan. Afghanistan's floor score rests on its formal gender-apartheid policies restricting women's education, work, and movement, now compounded by the first direct aerial assault it has launched into a neighboring country.
A judicial order in Pakistan for a commission on blasphemy-law misuse, due within 30 days, is a court directive rather than an executive reform, and does not on its own support an improved score.

sectorAlerts.sector: West Africa and the Sahel — Hunger Season Deepens Alongside Renewed Conflict
sectorAlerts.headline: Up to 54.8 million people across West Africa and the Sahel face acute hunger during the June-August lean season, with food-aid funding cut more than 45 percent in Niger and Mali; JNIM renews its Bamako offensive.
observations: Nigeria (18.0 of 100): roughly 35 million people face food insecurity, with the northeast the epicenter. This continues the already-priced crisis that produced the country's June 19 score; no new discrete event this cycle moved the score further.
Mali (12.5 of 100): JNIM fighters renewed a coordinated offensive on July 4 after a nearly two-month pause, and the group's blockade of fuel and food into Bamako continues.
A joint UN food-agency briefing on July 9 will update the regional hunger figure and confirm whether the funding cuts have translated into realized food-assistance reductions on the ground.

sectorAlerts.sector: United States — A Mixed Window on a Recently Set Critical-Band Score
sectorAlerts.headline: The United States, in the Critical band since June 9, sees a Supreme Court win on birthright citizenship offset by upheld asylum limits, Temporary Protected Status termination, and a Housing First policy rollback.
observations: The United States (17.5 of 100) enters tonight's assessment for the first time this cycle. The Supreme Court upheld birthright citizenship on June 30 — a judicial check on the executive branch, not a change in executive conduct — while separately upholding border "metering" limits on daily asylum applications and allowing termination of Temporary Protected Status for Haitian and Syrian nationals on June 25.

A separate housing-policy change caps rental-assistance funding for formerly homeless people at 30 percent of prior levels, down from about 87 percent, moving away from Housing First models. Advocates warn this could disrupt housing assistance for more than 170,000 people.

Neither direction was strong enough, on its own or combined, to move the score away from the baseline set less than a month ago. The realized impact of the housing-policy rollback is the next concrete checkpoint to watch.

methodologyNotes.name: A New, Earlier Screening Step Catches False Signals Before They Reach a Full Assessment
methodologyNotes.description: Tonight's scan introduces an earlier screening step that catches news signals before they ever reach a full country or company assessment. Five signals were screened out tonight: a lawsuit against Apple that had already been filed back in December 2024 and was not new; a routine legal filing by Midjourney that was procedural, not a welfare or safety issue; labor-rights findings about Gulf countries that were all older than the two-week window this benchmark tracks; forward-looking European Union AI rules that do not take effect until August 2 and had not yet produced any dated compliance action; and a milestone in Myanmar's death toll crossing 100,000, which was treated as a running total reaching a round number rather than a new single event. This is a new capability, not a step backward from the 15 signals caught in the four nights before this one — that earlier count measured a different thing, described in the next note.

methodologyNotes.name: How the Benchmark Continues to Separate Cause From Blame Across 14 of 15 Countries Tonight
methodologyNotes.description: Of the 15 countries assessed tonight, 14 had their scores confirmed using one of the benchmark's standard rules for telling who actually caused a harm: harm inflicted by one country on another is scored against the country that caused it, not the one it happened to (used for Pakistan, Lebanon, Afghanistan, and Ukraine tonight); harm caused by armed groups that are not the government is scored differently than harm caused by the government itself (used for Lebanon, Mali, and Sudan); harm from natural disasters or disease is scored based on how the government responded, not the disaster itself (used for Venezuela, Cuba, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo); and ongoing harm that was already factored into an existing score does not get counted twice just because it continues (used for Pakistan, China, Iran, and Nigeria). The United States was the one exception tonight — a genuinely mixed window of one favorable court ruling offset by several unfavorable policy actions did not fit neatly into any of these categories, and required a new kind of judgment call: neither direction was strong enough on its own to move the score.

methodologyNotes.name: A Data-Tracking Error for Cuba Was Found and Corrected
methodologyNotes.description: Cuba's internal working score had drifted to 27.8, but the correct, publicly published score has been 32.8 all along. This was caught and corrected before tonight's assessment began. The rule the benchmark follows is simple: the live, published score is always the authoritative one. No proposed or unconfirmed number was ever written into the public record.

methodologyNotes.name: The Mali-Burkina Faso Scoring Gap Is Formally Flagged as an Open Question
methodologyNotes.description: Mali and Burkina Faso are both run by military governments with comparably documented failures to protect civilians, yet Mali's score (12.5 of 100) sits nearly twice as high as Burkina Faso's (6.3 of 100). This gap was first raised on June 24 and has now gone 14 consecutive days without a decision from the benchmark's own coordinating team. By design, a single nightly check cannot resolve a question about whether two peer countries are being scored consistently against each other — that requires a dedicated review across the affected countries. This briefing formally names the gap as the longest-running open methodology question currently tracked, rather than repeating it as a routine footnote.

forwardTriggers.trigger: 14-day disaster-response checkpoint. Independent confirmation of the forensic pathologist's undercount allegation, or documented aid obstruction, would move the score toward a formal change from 18.0 of 100.
Close of the Khamenei state funeral window. A documented lethal crackdown on mourners or political rivals would be a new scored event from 2.5 of 100.
Joint UN food-agency briefing on the West Africa and Sahel hunger season, currently showing 54.8 million people at risk regionally.
Same joint UN food-agency briefing; the separate Mali-Burkina Faso calibration review remains open at the coordinator level with no set date.
Temporary Protected Status work authorization expires for roughly 350,000 Haitians in the United States. This is United States conduct, not scored against Haiti.
United States-Iran talks scheduled to resume in Doha. A verifiable agreement would be a positive scored event; a breakdown combined with new provocation would be a negative one.
Full reassessment due. Reversing the June 8 impunity law and releasing detained union leaders are the conditions that would move the score.
The natural seasonal peak for the current Ebola outbreak closes. Documented government obstruction of WHO response corridors is the trigger toward the absolute floor.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. No public compliance roadmap has been published yet.
The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable. Anthropic's compliance work is already documented.
The famine-risk window closes. A formal famine declaration combined with documented government aid obstruction would move the score.
The 2027 election under the July 2025 indefinite re-election amendment. Restoring due process and reinstating civil society are the conditions that would move the score back up.
Coordinator-level review of Mali's score against Burkina Faso's for comparable military-government conduct. Fourteen days open with no set review date.
First confirmed enforcement action under the new Ethnic Unity Law's diaspora-liability clause — a prosecution, forced return, or family detention abroad.
An implemented ceasefire with unobstructed humanitarian access at El Obeid is the only path to a positive scored event from the absolute floor.
A fourth nationwide blackout, or a documented healthcare-access failure beyond the current figure, is the next event to watch from 32.8 of 100.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: Mali's score has sat 6.2 points above Burkina Faso's for 14 days without a coordinator decision: what documented evidence threshold under the benchmark's cross-peer calibration review would close that gap and bring the two military governments' scores into alignment?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Two Sahel military governments show comparably documented state collapse, yet one score sits nearly twice the other's for two weeks running.
